summary:
Suleiman I, 'the Magnificent' (r. 1520–1566 AD), was the longest-reigning and most celebrated sultan of the [[event:2400|Ottoman Empire]], under whom the empire reached its territorial, institutional, and cultural apex — known to his own subjects as Kanuni ('the Lawgiver') for his comprehensive codification of Ottoman law, and to admiring Europeans as 'the Magnificent' for the splendor of his court and the scale of his military campaigns. During his 46-year reign, Suleiman expanded the empire to its maximum extent: conquering Belgrade (1521), Rhodes (1522), defeating Hungary at [[event:2402|Mohács]] (1526) and making it an Ottoman vassal, besieging [[event:2323|Vienna]] (1529), winning the naval Battle of Preveza (1538) against a Christian coalition securing Ottoman Mediterranean dominance, and conducting campaigns into Persia and North Africa.

Suleiman's domestic achievements were equally remarkable. His legal codes (the Kanunname) codified Ottoman administrative, criminal, and land law alongside Islamic sharia, creating a systematic legal framework — a rationalization of governance that Suleiman personally monitored and revised. His chief architect Sinan designed over 300 buildings during his career, including the Süleymaniye Mosque (1558) in Istanbul and the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne — architectural masterpieces that defined Ottoman high culture. Suleiman was himself a poet (composing under the name Muhibbi) and a goldsmith; his court attracted scholars, poets, and artists who made Istanbul a cultural center rivaling any European capital. His personal life was extraordinary: his deviation from Ottoman tradition by formally marrying his Ukrainian slave-concubine Hürrem Sultan (known in Europe as Roxelana), who exercised unprecedented political influence, and the tragic fratricide among his sons that his succession struggles produced, became the subject of enduring historical fascination.
